title: Film
intro: Film questions in quizbowl distribute across a canon of national movements and their defining directors. The French New Wave (Godard, Truffaut, Rivette, Varda) and Italian Neorealism (De Sica, Rossellini, later Fellini and Antonioni) anchor postwar European cinema, while a broader European art-cinema cluster covers Bergman, Bunuel, Dreyer, Fassbinder, Herzog, Tarkovsky, and Kieslowski across Sweden, Spain, Germany, and Eastern Europe. Classic Hollywood supplies its own dense cluster — Hitchcock, Welles, Wilder, Capra — organized around film noir and the studio-era star system, with Citizen Kane, Casablanca, and Vertigo recurring as both standalone answers and supporting clues.

American cinema after the studio era splits into two clusters: New Hollywood auteurs (Scorsese, Kubrick, Coppola) who reshaped genre filmmaking in the late 1960s and 1970s with The Godfather, Taxi Driver, and Apocalypse Now, and an independent/experimental strand running from Cassavetes and Maya Deren through Lynch, Tarantino, and Spike Lee to more recent figures like Sofia Coppola and Paul Thomas Anderson. East Asian cinema forms another major cluster, led by Kurosawa's samurai films, Ozu's family dramas, Wong Kar-wai's romantic style, and the Korean New Wave (Bong Joon-ho, Park Chan-wook).

A smaller but consistent share of questions covers world cinema beyond these poles — Satyajit Ray and Indian Parallel Cinema, Iranian art-house directors like Kiarostami, African cinema through Ousmane Sembene, and scattered national new waves (Taiwanese, Romanian, Czechoslovak). Genre film — westerns, movie musicals, epics, and horror — appears as its own recurring cluster independent of national movement, alongside film theory and technique (montage, auteur theory, the male gaze, the Cannes and Venice festivals). Countries and cities function as organizing answerlines in their own right, gathering clues about the directors and movements associated with them rather than any single film.

sections.name: Silent Film, German Expressionism, and Soviet Montage
sections.blurb: Early cinema's foundational silent-era movements, from slapstick to Expressionist shadow to Soviet editing theory.

entries.note: British-born silent film star and director of the Tramp character; directed and starred in The Great Dictator and Modern Times.
Soviet director-theorist of montage editing; directed Battleship Potemkin, famed for its Odessa Steps sequence.
German Expressionist director who later worked in Hollywood film noir; directed Metropolis and M.
American silent-era physical comedian and director known for deadpan stunts; starred in The General.
Eisenstein's silent Soviet propaganda film famed for its Odessa Steps montage sequence.
Murnau's unauthorized silent adaptation of Dracula, featuring the vampire Count Orlok.
German Expressionist silent film with distorted, painted sets, framed as a madman's asylum story.
Chaplin's sound-era satire in which he plays both a Jewish barber and a Hitler-parodying dictator.
Soviet documentarian and montage theorist who directed the experimental Man with a Movie Camera.
Vertov's experimental Soviet documentary showcasing city life through innovative montage and self-reflexive technique.

sections.name: Classic Hollywood and Film Noir
sections.blurb: The studio-era system, its stars and genres, and the shadowy crime films it produced.

entries.note: American director-actor whose Citizen Kane pioneered deep-focus cinematography; also directed Touch of Evil.
British-American "Master of Suspense" director of Vertigo, Psycho, and Rear Window.
Shadowy, morally ambiguous crime genre of 1940s-50s Hollywood, marked by chiaroscuro lighting and femme fatales.
Hitchcock's psychological thriller about a detective's obsessive pursuit of a woman he believes is dead, plagued by acrophobia.
Welles's landmark film about newspaper tycoon Charles Foster Kane, told through flashbacks probing the mystery of "Rosebud."
German-American actress known for sultry roles in The Blue Angel and Josef von Sternberg's films.
American writer-director of fast-talking screwball comedies like Sullivan's Travels and The Lady Eve.
Billy Wilder's noir about a faded silent film star, Norma Desmond, who ensnares a struggling screenwriter.
Actress known for Roman Holiday and Breakfast at Tiffany's, a defining star of 1950s-60s Hollywood.
American actress known for femme fatale roles, notably in Double Indemnity.
Austrian-American director of sharp-witted films across noir and comedy; directed Double Indemnity and Some Like It Hot.
Wartime romance set in Vichy-controlled Morocco, starring Humphrey Bogart and Ingrid Bergman.
German-American director of glossy Technicolor melodramas like All That Heaven Allows and Imitation of Life.
German-American director known for sophisticated romantic comedies with the "Lubitsch touch"; directed Ninotchka.
American director of populist, sentimental films like It's a Wonderful Life and Mr. Smith Goes to Washington.
Hitchcock's thriller about a wheelchair-bound photographer who suspects a neighbor of murder while watching through his window.
Hitchcock's thriller in which a California coastal town is besieged by unexplained, violent bird attacks.

sections.name: Italian Neorealism and Postwar Italian Cinema
sections.blurb: Postwar Italy's turn to street-level realism and its evolution into Fellini's and Antonioni's art cinema.

entries.note: Italian director who moved from neorealism to surreal autobiographical spectacle; directed La Dolce Vita and 8½.
Italian director of alienation-themed art films; directed L'Avventura and Blow-Up.
Italian Neorealist director of Bicycle Thieves, following a poor father's search for his stolen bicycle.
Fellini's episodic film following a tabloid journalist through Rome's decadent high society.
Refers to Roberto Rossellini, Italian Neorealist director of Rome, Open City and Paisan.
Fellini's self-reflexive film about a director suffering creative block while making his next picture.
De Sica's Neorealist film about a poor father searching Rome for his stolen bicycle needed for work.
Italian actor and frequent Fellini collaborator, starring in La Dolce Vita and 8½.
Italian director-poet known for provocative, politically charged films like Salo and The Gospel According to St. Matthew.
Italian Neorealist director of Rome, Open City and Paisan, chronicling wartime and postwar Italy.

sections.name: French New Wave and French Cinema
sections.blurb: The critics-turned-directors who broke studio convention, and the French tradition around them.

entries.note: Leading French New Wave director known for jump cuts and self-reflexive style; directed Breathless and Weekend.
French New Wave director and critic; directed The 400 Blows and Jules et Jim.
Late-1950s movement of young critics-turned-directors (Godard, Truffaut, Rivette) rejecting studio convention for personal, self-reflexive filmmaking.
French New Wave critic-turned-director known for long, labyrinthine films like Celine and Julie Go Boating.
French New Wave-adjacent director often called its "grandmother"; directed Cleo from 5 to 7.
Godard's jump-cut-driven French New Wave crime film about a petty criminal and his American girlfriend.
Varda's real-time film following a singer awaiting cancer test results through Paris.
French poetic-realist director and forerunner of the New Wave; directed The Rules of the Game and Grand Illusion.
Truffaut's semi-autobiographical film following troubled youth Antoine Doinel, ending on a famous freeze-frame.
Jean Renoir's satirical French ensemble drama about aristocrats and servants at a country estate, exposing prewar social hypocrisy.

sections.name: European Art Cinema
sections.blurb: Postwar auteurs across Sweden, Spain, Germany, and Eastern Europe pursuing personal, philosophical filmmaking.

entries.note: Swedish director of stark psychological and existential dramas; directed Persona, The Seventh Seal, and Wild Strawberries.
Spanish Surrealist director who collaborated with Salvador Dali on Un Chien Andalou; later worked in Mexico and France.
Prolific New German Cinema director known for melodramas critiquing postwar West German society.
Bergman's experimental film about a mute actress and her nurse whose identities blur.
Danish director known for austere, spiritually intense films; directed The Passion of Joan of Arc.
New German Cinema director known for obsessive protagonists and jungle epics like Fitzcarraldo and Aguirre.
Soviet director of slow, meditative, image-driven films; directed Andrei Rublev, Solaris, and Stalker.
Bela Tarr's over seven-hour Hungarian slow-cinema epic shot in stark black and white.
Chantal Akerman's minimalist film observing a widow's domestic routine over three days.
Spanish Surrealist painter who co-wrote and co-directed Un Chien Andalou with Luis Bunuel.

entries.topic: The Passion of Joan of Arc
entries.answerline: The Passion of Joan of Arc
entries.note: Dreyer's silent film using intense close-ups to depict Joan of Arc's trial and execution.
Refers to Kieslowski's Three Colors: Blue, following a widow processing grief after her husband and daughter's deaths.
French Surrealist writer-director known for Beauty and the Beast and Orpheus.
Polish director of the Three Colors trilogy and The Decalogue, exploring moral and existential themes.
Danish director and Dogme 95 co-founder known for provocative films like Melancholia and Dancer in the Dark.
Czech New Wave director who later worked in Hollywood; directed One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest and Amadeus.
Spanish director of vividly colored melodramas exploring gender and desire; directed Talk to Her and All About My Mother.

entries.topic: Portrait of a Lady on Fire
entries.answerline: Portrait of a Lady on Fire
entries.note: Celine Sciamma's French period drama about a painter falling for the noblewoman she has been secretly hired to paint.
French director known for austere, minimalist style using non-professional actors; directed Au Hasard Balthazar.
Film movement rendering dreamlike, irrational imagery, launched by Bunuel and Dali's Un Chien Andalou.
Dario Argento's Italian giallo horror film about an American ballet student uncovering a coven at her German dance academy.
Bunuel and Dali's Surrealist short film famous for its shocking eyeball-slicing opening image.

sections.name: New Hollywood
sections.blurb: The late-1960s to 1970s American renaissance of director-driven, genre-subverting studio films.

entries.note: American director of gritty crime and character studies; directed Taxi Driver, Goodfellas, and Raging Bull.
Meticulous American auteur working across genres; directed 2001: A Space Odyssey, A Clockwork Orange, and Barry Lyndon.
Scorsese's film starring Robert De Niro as alienated cabbie Travis Bickle, who plots to assassinate a senator and rescue a child prostitute.
Arthur Penn's crime film about the Depression-era outlaw couple, a landmark of graphic violence in American cinema.
Kubrick's candlelit period drama following an Irish rogue's rise through 18th-century society.
Coppola's Mafia epic following the Corleone family, starring Marlon Brando and Al Pacino.
Kubrick's science fiction epic featuring the rogue AI HAL 9000 and a mysterious monolith guiding human evolution.
Kubrick's dystopian adaptation following ultraviolent delinquent Alex, subjected to the aversion-based Ludovico Technique.
Coppola's Vietnam War epic loosely adapting Heart of Darkness, following a mission to assassinate the renegade Colonel Kurtz.
Steve McQueen action film famed for its extended San Francisco car chase sequence.
Polanski's neo-noir following detective Jake Gittes into a web of Los Angeles water rights corruption.
American director of the Godfather trilogy and the Vietnam War epic Apocalypse Now.
Mike Nichols's film about a directionless college graduate's affair with an older woman, Mrs. Robinson, and her daughter.

sections.name: American Independent and Experimental Cinema
sections.blurb: Avant-garde, DIY, and post-studio American filmmaking outside the major genre pipeline.

entries.note: American surrealist director of unsettling, dreamlike narratives; directed Eraserhead, Blue Velvet, and Mulholland Drive.
American director exploring race and urban life; directed Do the Right Thing and BlacKkKlansman.
American writer-director of neurotic comedies; directed Annie Hall and Manhattan.
Pop artist who also made avant-garde experimental films like Sleep and Empire.
Spike Lee's film depicting rising racial tension on a Brooklyn block during a heat wave.
American independent film pioneer known for raw, improvisational dramas like Faces and A Woman Under the Influence.
Ukrainian-American avant-garde filmmaker known for the surreal short Meshes of the Afternoon.
American director known for nonlinear structure and genre pastiche; directed Pulp Fiction and Kill Bill.
American director known for poetic, nature-focused visuals and voiceover; directed Days of Heaven and The Tree of Life.

entries.topic: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
entries.answerline: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
entries.note: Michel Gondry film written by Charlie Kaufman about a couple who erase each other from their memories.
American director known for atmospheric, mood-driven films like Lost in Translation and The Virgin Suicides.
Paul Thomas Anderson's film following ruthless oilman Daniel Plainview's rise in early-20th-century California.
Michael Snow's structural film consisting of a single 45-minute zoom across a room.

sections.name: East Asian Cinema
sections.blurb: Japan, Hong Kong, South Korea, Taiwan, and China's directors, genres, and national film movements.

entries.note: Japanese director of samurai epics blending Western influence with jidaigeki tradition; directed Rashomon, Seven Samurai, and Yojimbo.
South Korean director blending genre and social critique; directed Parasite and Memories of Murder.
Hong Kong director of romantic, visually stylized films; directed Chungking Express and In the Mood for Love.
Warrior figures central to Japanese jidaigeki films, epitomized by Kurosawa's Seven Samurai and Yojimbo.
Wong Kar-wai's dual-story Hong Kong romance shot in a kinetic, impressionistic style.
Kurosawa's late epic adapting King Lear to feudal Japan, depicting a warlord's sons warring over his kingdom.
Japanese actor and frequent Kurosawa collaborator, starring in Seven Samurai, Yojimbo, and Rashomon.
Chinese martial-arts genre of chivalrous swordfighters, exemplified by Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon.
Japanese director known for quiet family dramas shot with a static, low "tatami" camera; directed Tokyo Story.
Edward Yang's Taiwanese family drama following three generations of a Taipei household.
Taiwanese-American director spanning wuxia (Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon) and American drama (Brokeback Mountain).
Japanese Studio Ghibli director of animated films like Spirited Away and My Neighbor Totoro.
Wong Kar-wai's Hong Kong drama about two neighbors who bond over their spouses' affair.
Bong Joon-ho's class-satire thriller about a poor family infiltrating a wealthy household; first non-English-language film to win Best Picture.
South Korean director known for stylish, violent thrillers; directed Oldboy and The Handmaiden.
Kurosawa's film presenting a murder and assault from four contradictory eyewitness perspectives.
Kurosawa's film about a masterless samurai who manipulates two rival gangs against each other in a small town.

sections.name: World and Global Cinema
sections.blurb: Directors and national cinemas from South Asia, the Middle East, Africa, and Latin America.

entries.note: Bengali director of the Apu Trilogy, a leading figure of Indian Parallel Cinema.
Iranian director known for minimalist, self-reflexive films; directed Close-Up and Taste of Cherry.
Kiarostami's docufiction blending real events and reenactment about a man who impersonated a famous director.
New Zealand director known for The Piano and Power of the Dog; second woman to win the Palme d'Or.
Chilean-French surrealist director of midnight-movie cult films like El Topo and The Holy Mountain.
French-Czech animated film depicting enslaved humans on an alien planet ruled by giant blue creatures.
Senegalese "father of African cinema," director of Xala and Black Girl, also a novelist.
Senegalese film by Djibril Diop Mambety following a young couple dreaming of escaping Dakar for Paris.
Apichatpong Weerasethakul's Thai film about a dying man visited by ghosts and past lives, winner of the Palme d'Or.

sections.name: Genre Cinema
sections.blurb: Popular genres — westerns, musicals, epics, horror, and science fiction — spanning eras and countries.

entries.note: American genre of frontier gunslinger films, from John Ford's classics to Sergio Leone's spaghetti westerns.
Western shot in near real time about a marshal facing outlaws alone as the town's citizens refuse to help.
Bob Fosse's autobiographical musical about a workaholic director-choreographer confronting mortality.
American director who defined the Western genre with Monument Valley landscapes; directed Stagecoach and The Searchers.
David Lean's sweeping desert epic dramatizing T.E. Lawrence's role in the Arab Revolt against the Ottomans.
Depression-era backstage musical with Busby Berkeley choreography, credited with reviving the film musical genre.
American director-choreographer of movie musicals; directed Cabaret and All That Jazz.
The Wizard of Oz's young protagonist, swept to the magical land of Oz by a Kansas tornado.
Howard Hawks musical comedy starring Marilyn Monroe as a gold-digging showgirl singing "Diamonds Are a Girl's Best Friend."
Genre integrating song and dance into narrative, from Golden Age Hollywood spectacles to Bob Fosse's darker takes.
Italian director who created the "Spaghetti Western" with the Dollars Trilogy and Once Upon a Time in the West.
Wachowskis' science fiction film about a hacker discovering reality is a simulated construct controlled by machines.

sections.name: Film Theory, Technique, and Festivals
sections.blurb: Critics, theorists, editing and camera techniques, and the festivals that anoint prestige cinema.

entries.note: Prestigious annual French festival awarding the Palme d'Or, a major launching point for international art cinema.
Editing technique juxtaposing shots to create meaning, theorized and pioneered by Soviet directors like Eisenstein.
French film critic and theorist who championed realism and mentored the future French New Wave directors.
American critic who popularized auteur theory in the United States.
Frame-by-frame film medium spanning Disney features, Miyazaki's Studio Ghibli, and stop-motion techniques.
Camera technique tilting the frame off the horizontal axis to convey unease, associated with German Expressionism and noir.
Post-production process of assembling shots, foundational to both montage theory and continuity style.
British feminist film theorist who coined "the male gaze" in her essay on visual pleasure and narrative cinema.

sections.name: Countries, Places, and Motifs
sections.blurb: Nations, cities, and recurring images that organize clues across multiple films and directors.

entries.note: Home to the French New Wave, the Cannes Film Festival, and directors Godard, Truffaut, and Renoir.
Home to the Korean New Wave, directors Bong Joon-ho and Park Chan-wook, and the Oscar-winning Parasite.
Home to Italian Neorealism, Fellini, Antonioni, and the spaghetti westerns of Sergio Leone.
Home to Wong Kar-wai's romantic dramas and a thriving wuxia and action film industry.
Home to acclaimed art-house directors like Abbas Kiarostami and Jafar Panahi, known for minimalist, self-reflexive films.
Home to Soviet montage cinema and directors Eisenstein and Tarkovsky.
Home to directors like David Cronenberg and Denis Villeneuve.
Home to director Bela Tarr, known for long-take slow cinema like Satantango.
Home to acclaimed art-house directors like Abbas Kiarostami and Jafar Panahi, known for minimalist, self-reflexive films.
Home to Luis Bunuel's later work and directors Guillermo del Toro and Alejandro Gonzalez Inarritu.
Recurring cinematic image from the Lumiere brothers' early Arrival of a Train to numerous later thrillers.
Home to Cinema Novo directors like Glauber Rocha.
Home to the Czechoslovak New Wave and directors like Milos Forman.
Vampire figure adapted across numerous films, from Nosferatu to Bela Lugosi's 1931 Universal picture.
Home to Kurosawa, Ozu, and the jidaigeki samurai film tradition.
Home to Ingmar Bergman's stark, existential dramas.
Central image of De Sica's Bicycle Thieves, symbolizing postwar Italian poverty.
Central image of De Sica's Bicycle Thieves, symbolizing postwar Italian poverty.
Home to Satyajit Ray's Parallel Cinema and the mass-market Bollywood industry.
Subject of Dreyer's The Passion of Joan of Arc, known for its close-up-driven depiction of her trial.
Setting and hub of the French New Wave and the French film industry.
Home to director Lav Diaz, known for extremely long-duration films.
Home to the Romanian New Wave, known for austere social-realist films like 4 Months, 3 Weeks and 2 Days.
Home to the Taiwanese New Wave and directors Ang Lee and Edward Yang.
Home to New Argentine Cinema directors of the 1990s-2000s.
American musician subject of documentaries and biopics exploring his career and mythology.
Hindi-language film industry based in Mumbai, known for musical numbers and melodrama.
Home to the Fifth Generation directors and a fast-growing contemporary film industry.
Refers to China, home to the Fifth Generation directors and a fast-growing contemporary film industry.
Recurring thematic concern across art cinema, from Bergman's personified Death in The Seventh Seal to countless narrative climaxes.
Home to a major Arab-world film industry centered in Cairo.
Home to New German Cinema directors Fassbinder, Herzog, and Wenders.
Home to German Expressionism, New German Cinema, and directors Lang, Fassbinder, and Herzog.
Recurring color motif in film cinematography and production design, from The Matrix's code to noir lighting schemes.
Subject of the documentary The Act of Killing, examining perpetrators of 1960s anti-communist massacres.
American writer whose life and essays inspired the documentary I Am Not Your Negro and the film If Beale Street Could Talk.
Disney's iconic animated mascot, introduced in the short Steamboat Willie.
Setting and production hub for numerous films, including Scorsese's and Woody Allen's work.
Setting of Italian Neorealism's landmark films and Fellini's La Dolce Vita.
Home to Ousmane Sembene, the "father of African cinema."
Home to Surrealists Bunuel and Dali and contemporary director Pedro Almodovar.
Home to Soviet montage cinema and directors Eisenstein and Tarkovsky.
Horror figure central to Nosferatu, Dracula adaptations, and countless later genre films.
Home to the Venice Film Festival, the oldest film festival in the world.
Historical backdrop for Italian Neorealism and countless war films across national cinemas.

appendix.answer: the bridge on the River Kwai
The Good, the Bad and the Ugly
The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly
